clean_story:
Sun at center. Planets in orbit.
The names Copernicus and Kepler mark the two steps that untangled the solar system’s design. Copernicus first moved Earth from the universe’s center—a radical shift like realizing a stage revolves around the audience, not the actor. Kepler then proved the orbits weren’t perfect circles but stretched loops, like a hula hoop pinched into an oval.

Copernicus, a Polish astronomer, spent decades calculating a sun-centered model (De Revolutionibus, 1543), though he kept it quiet until his death—fearing backlash. His math was clunky, still using old tools like Ptolemy’s "epicycles" (tiny wheels on wheels to explain planetary wobbles). Then came Kepler, a German mathematician hired to analyze Tycho Brahe’s precise star logs. After years of failed circles, he tested an ellipse—a flattened circle—and suddenly Mars’ path fit. His three laws (1609–1619) revealed planets speed up near the sun and slow farther out, like a swing gaining momentum at the low point.

Without telescopes, they relied on naked-eye data and stubborn math. Copernicus’ sun-center laid the foundation; Kepler’s ellipses made it work. Newton later used these laws to define gravity.

Three related subjects are pre-telescope astronomy, Tycho Brahe’s star catalogs, and how math predicts unseen worlds.
